title: Industrial Growth in Tamil Nadu: Then and Now!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: This recovered chapter begins without a printed salutation. The Tamil reading copy repairs only source-confirmed or unambiguous OCR errors and page-break fractures. Financial figures damaged in OCR were checked against the source images; the canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.

It is widely said that industries that ought to come to Tamil Nadu are marching away to other States; newspapers too have reported it. Reports say that investments worth 12,000 crore rupees by industrialists from the Kongu region are set to move to Karnataka; that the principal reason is the worsening investment climate in Tamil Nadu; and that, if this continues, industries from other parts of Tamil Nadu may also migrate to other States.

The Karnataka Government is developing an industrial zone over 1,400 acres in Chamarajanagar district on the Tamil Nadu-Karnataka border. To attract investors, a "Chamarajanagar Investors' Conference" was held in Coimbatore on the 20th of this month. Conducted by the Federation of Karnataka Chambers of Commerce and Industry, the conference drew the attention and interest of many entrepreneurs from Tamil Nadu's western districts. Karnataka's Chief Minister, Industries Minister and senior State officials attended and showered industrialists with promises. Speaking there, the Karnataka Chief Minister invited Tamil Nadu's industrialists: "Come and start industries in Karnataka; we will provide every facility." To counter that invitation, perhaps our Chief Minister Jayalalithaa, while staying at Kodanad near Coimbatore itself, is issuing an invitation to Karnataka's industrialists through a "video conference"!

As a result of the Karnataka conference in Coimbatore, about two hundred industrialists operating in the Kongu region, including Coimbatore, Tiruppur, Erode and Salem, have come forward to invest 12,000 crore rupees and establish industries in Chamarajanagar.

Speaking about the conference, Federation Assistant Secretary Vijayakumar has boasted: "The Coimbatore conference was a tremendous success. It gives us great happiness that, in a single day, industrialists from Tamil Nadu came forward to invest more than 12,000 crore rupees in the Chamarajanagar industrial zone." He further said: "In particular, an export company from Tiruppur has agreed to invest 2,000 crore rupees. A woman industrialist from Coimbatore has expressed her desire to invest 1,000 crore rupees in the zone. We will provide every facility to all those who come forward to do business in Karnataka and give them every assistance needed for industrial development."

The reasons investments are moving from Tamil Nadu to Karnataka are said to include power cuts, failures of government administration, inadequate infrastructure, the inability to meet those in power, and an approach that does not encourage industrial growth. Since the AIADMK assumed office, hundreds of industrial establishments in Tamil Nadu have closed, and tens of thousands of workers have gone to neighbouring States in search of a livelihood. Now even the industries that remain have begun moving out of the State.

Yet last year, after the Industries Department's demand for grants was debated in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa made several announcements under Rule 110 on behalf of the department. To please her, the Communist Party of India offered the praise: "With the Chief Minister's announcements, it would be no surprise if Tamil Nadu became the Manchester of India." Industries Minister Mohan said: "Our Amma is capable of accomplishing the impossible. She is the one who chased away even the tsunami. She is announcing such fine schemes so that Tamil Nadu may earn the name of the foremost State. She is bringing light." What do you make of the minister's statement? Is not the Karnataka Government's conference in Coimbatore itself fitting evidence of the extraordinary labour through which the Chief Minister is making Tamil Nadu the foremost State?

The Speaker put the finishing touch: "Our Amma, who labours day and night for Tamil Nadu's advancement, has issued these new announcements to take the State still further forward in industry. She has come as the morning star and is bringing light to Tamil Nadu."
Do you know what industrial projects the Chief Minister announced that day?

"A deep-sea port, a floating liquefied natural gas terminal, and a 500-megawatt gas-based power plant will be implemented in Nagapattinam district with an investment of 3,500 crore rupees; nine new industrial parks will be established in nine districts; and SIDCO will create a multi-storey industrial complex at Thirumazhisai."
The announcements continue in this fashion. But when I read the announcement about the multi-storey industrial complex at Thirumazhisai, a doubt arose!

After the AIADMK came to power in 2011, the Chief Minister read a statement under the same Rule 110 on 9 September 2011. Announcing that housing would be built on 311 acres at Thirumazhisai and that a satellite town would be established near Chennai, it was published prominently in every newspaper. But even before the Chief Minister made that announcement, the project had appeared in the Housing Minister R. Vaithilingam's policy note placed before the Assembly on 25 August 2011. The Chief Minister later read the same proposal as an announcement under Rule 110. I sought an explanation last year. The explanation is still being prepared! This is how industry "grows" under AIADMK rule. Let me now explain how this department functioned under the DMK government.

To assist Tamil Nadu's industrial and commercial growth, a Special Task Force comprising representatives of industrial and trade federations was constituted under the Chief Minister's leadership on 8 November 2006.

For the first time in India, Tamil Nadu formulated a separate policy for micro, small and medium enterprises in 2008. Its objectives included increasing their competitiveness and progressively improving their efficiency; encouraging agro-based industries to provide farmers additional income; and setting an annual growth target above ten per cent for micro, small and medium enterprises.

Before preparing the Industries Department's demand for grants for 2009-10, younger brother M. K. Stalin, then Deputy Chief Minister, convened a meeting at the Chennai Secretariat on 13 June 2009 of all federations of micro, small, medium and large entrepreneurs in Tamil Nadu. They discussed measures needed for the State's industrial growth. Similar meetings were subsequently held in Tirunelveli, Coimbatore, Tiruchi and Erode.

In 2011, the Union Government's Central Statistical Organisation published its Annual Survey of Industries for 2008-09. According to the survey: "Tamil Nadu has a total of 26,122 factories, placing it first in India by number of factories. Tamil Nadu also ranks first in the number of factory workers: 17,74,019 people work in factories in the State. Tamil Nadu ranks third in total investment, third in total industrial output, and third among States in factory net value added."

In its issue dated 8 July 2010, The Wall Street Journal highly praised the Tamil Nadu Government's performance. It wrote: "Tamil Nadu performs better than other Indian States in providing the land, roads and electricity needed by automobile manufacturing plants. It also provides, at a single location through a single-window system, more than twelve government approvals required to start and expand industries."

The Centre for Monitoring Indian Economy stated in its report for the October-December 2010 quarter that Tamil Nadu ranked third in industrial growth in India. According to it, at the beginning of AIADMK rule in 2001, that is, up to May 2001, proposed industrial investment in Tamil Nadu stood at 1,83,049 crore and 37 lakh rupees. By May 2006, at the end of five years of AIADMK rule, it had risen by only about 8,742 crore rupees, to 1,91,791 crore and 98 lakh rupees.

But after the DMK formed the government in 2006, it framed a new industrial policy and encouraged industrial growth; adopted a transparent and industry-friendly approach towards investors; and developed industrial infrastructure to global standards. As a result, proposed industrial investment increased by 5,73,765 crore and 94 lakh rupees over five years. By December 2010, total investment had risen nearly fourfold to 7,65,557 crore and 92 lakh rupees. A silent revolution had taken place in industrial development.
I have assembled here an account of how industry grew under the DMK government and how it is "growing" now. Let me give one example.

Last week, Coir Board Vice-Chairman K. Rayar met reporters in Chennai and said: "The Union Ministry of Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises, through the Central Coir Board, has a plan to establish a coir industrial park in Tamil Nadu with an investment of 100 crore rupees and financial assistance from the Union and State Governments. The Union Government is prepared to provide 70 crore rupees. The State Government needs to contribute only 10 crore. If the Khadi and Village Sarvodaya Sangam provides 20 crore rupees, the industrial park can be established over 200 acres at Vazhudareddy village in Villupuram. But the Tamil Nadu Government has not yet granted permission. For the past two months, I have tried but failed to meet Tamil Nadu Rural Industries Minister Mohan about this matter. One can easily meet even the President of our country and take a photograph with him. But even I, an official of the Union Government, cannot meet a Tamil Nadu minister. In Tamil Nadu, coconut husks and fibre worth 500 crore rupees are burned and wasted every year." Newspapers published this interview in which a Union Government official accused the Tamil Nadu Government. A week has passed. What is the Tamil Nadu Government's answer?
With affection,
M. K.
29 January 2014
